Now that we know that scarcity is amplified by loss framing and competition, its final amplifier is exclusive information. So here's another important thing to know about the principle of scarcity. It doesn't just work for commodities. It also works for information. One that's exclusive, that not everyone has access to, is more persuasive than already widespread information. The best evidence I have for this comes from a study done by a student of mine, an unusual student who started a successful business in Arizona importing beef into the United States and then selling it to large supermarket concerns. He came back to school to get his PhD and I was sitting on his doctoral committee. He wanted to do a study using his own business, his own customers, his own salespeople, his own industry. We began to talk about the research that showed first, people are more attracted to scarce commodities and second, they're more persuaded by exclusive information. So he decided he would do the following study. He went to his salespeople who called their customers on the phone. He said, divide your customers into three groups, then call them and tell them about the new allotment of beef we've just received. For the first group of customers, use our standard sales appeal, which was, we have a certain allotment of beef at such and such a price and at such and such a quality and we can guarantee delivery by such and such a date. How many carloads of beef would you like? And then he said, call the second group and add, by the way, because of certain weather conditions in Australia, there's likely to be a shortage of Australian beef in the near future. How many carloads would you like? So the salespeople added scarcity to the mix for the second group of customers. Adding scarcity more than doubled the number of carloads of beef sold from 10 to 24. It was the third group of customers that was most interesting. They were called on the phone and told about the allotment of beef as well as about the future scarcity and then they were told, and this information comes to us from our exclusive sources in the Australian National Weather Service. No one else has this information yet. This was the scarcity double whammy. Not only was the beef scarce, the information that the beef was scarce was scarce. What resulted? Six times as much beef was purchased over the usual sales appeal and this man, my student, came to me afterwards and said, you know, Dr. Chaldini, all of this was true. There was an impending shortage of Australian beef. This information did come to us through our exclusive contacts in the Australian National Weather Service. We just never knew to use it before. Because he didn't know what the research said. We now know what the research says and we'd be bunglers of scarcity and exclusivity if we didn't honestly incorporate it into our messaging.
